title: Making a simple timber sawhorse
subtitle: Build a sturdy pair of sawhorses from construction timber for the workshop
excerpt: How to make a pair of simple timber sawhorses from 75 x 50 mm structural softwood. Covers the leg geometry, the notched top rail, and the cross-brace that keeps each horse from racking under load.
sourceNotes: Traditional workshop construction practice. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain). Stanley Tools workshop guide (reference format).

glossaryTerms.definition: The outward angle at which sawhorse legs are cut and fitted. A splay of 15 degrees from vertical gives a stable base without the horse being so wide that it becomes awkward. The top of the leg is cut square; the foot is cut at the splay angle so it sits flat on the floor. The leg joint into the top rail is also cut at the splay angle.

content.text: A pair of sawhorses at 900 mm high is the most-used workshop accessory: they support timber for sawing, panels for painting, and doors for planing. A good sawhorse takes 30 minutes to make and lasts a decade. The key dimensions are the height and the

. This version uses a single notched top rail and four splayed legs; the cross-brace running across each pair of legs prevents racking under side load. Materials are standard construction softwood; one horse requires roughly 5 m of 75 x 50 mm CLS (constructional lumber sawn) or equivalent.

For a pair of horses: 10 m of 75 x 50 mm PSE softwood. 65 mm wood screws (20 off). A circular saw (set to 15-degree bevel for the leg cuts). A cordless drill. A combination square. A tape measure. A carpenter's pencil.

Cut one top rail to 900 mm. Mark the leg positions on its underside at 100 mm from each end. Use the combination square to mark the 15-degree splay angle at each leg mark in both directions; in plan (legs splayed outward along the length of the top rail) and in profile (legs splayed outward on the width of the top rail). Notch the top rail to half its depth at each leg position using the circular saw and a chisel, cutting the notch at the 15-degree angle so the leg top sits flush into the notch when splayed.

Set the circular saw blade to 15 degrees. Each leg will be approximately 850 mm long (depending on the height you want; adjust from a horse height of 900 mm, less the depth of the top rail). Make the top cut square (90 degrees in plan, 15 degrees in profile) so the leg fits into the notch. Make the foot cut parallel to the floor, also at 15 degrees. The foot of each leg must sit flat.

Press each leg into its notch in the top rail. Drill two pilot holes through the side of the top rail into the leg at each joint and drive 65 mm screws. Check the horse stands level on a flat floor and that all four feet contact the ground. Add a cross-brace of 75 x 25 mm timber between each pair of legs, roughly 200 mm from the floor, screwed at each end; this prevents the horse racking sideways under load.

A folding sawhorse uses hinged legs and a folding brace so it stores flat; the hardware adds a small extra cost per horse but saves significant storage space. For heavy panel work, increase the top rail to 100 x 50 mm and the leg timber to the same section. Horses for painting can be fitted with a 50 mm ledge screwed to each side of the top rail to prevent work rolling off.

attrs.symptom: Horse rocks on the floor — one foot does not contact the ground
attrs.cause: Leg foot cuts are not parallel, or the splay angles are inconsistent across the four legs.
attrs.fix: Place the horse on a flat surface and identify the short leg. Trim the opposite leg foot slightly or shim the short leg with a felt pad to level the horse.
